children.value: Integration reverses differentiation, and every antiderivative comes with an arbitrary constant

children.html: CCC

children.value: The definite integral is the limit of a sum of rectangle areas, giving the net area under a curve.
This theorem ties differentiation and integration together and is the workhorse for evaluating definite integrals.
Substitution, integration by parts, and trigonometric identities are the tools you will use to evaluate the integrals that appear throughout quantum mechanics.

children.html: F(x)=∫f(x) dxF′(x)=f(x)F(x) = \displaystyle\int f(x) \ dx \newline F'(x) = f(x)F(x)=∫f(x) dxF′(x)=f(x)

children.value: Reversing the bounds changes the sign of definite integrals
